column6: The Role of Streamflow and Land Use in Limiting Oversummer Survival of Juvenile Steelhead in California Streams
column10: instream shelter (e.g., woody debris, rootwads, overhanging banks), riparian vegetation
column11: qualitative score assignment between 1 (low summer habitat suitability) and 3 (high summer habitat suitability) based on instream shelter and riparian canopy cover, which was then collapsed into "low" (1 and 2 scores) and "high" (3 scores); pool vs. riffle categorial variable included separately
column12: repeated seine counts in isolated habitat units; apparent oversummer survival probability was modeled as a response variable to late-summer count ~ binomial (early-summer count, survival probability)
column13: cover had a positive association with oversummer survival; habitat suitability coefficient was 0.369 and 95% credible interval didn't include 0; also had a multiplicative effect with a 31% increase in survival in high-suitability units and a mean effect of 1.31 (95% CI 1.17-1.47); pools had a small positive effect relative to riffles
column14: summer streamflow magnitude (positive), vineyard land use (negative), cover (positive), density dependence (negative)
column15: yes - straightforwardly relevant, directly evaluates impact of cover on juvenile rearing/presence

column6: Comparisons of Habitat Availability and Habitat Use by an Allopatric Cohort of Juvenile Atlantic Salmon Salmo salar under Conditions of Low Competition in a Norwegian Stream
column10: submerged structures (excluding substract), undercut banks/overhanging vegetation, broken water surface (surface turbulence); substrate size included as an independent habitat variable (not as cover)
column11: rope method stretched across transects with 20-cm intervals, assessing the percent obscured from overhead view in 10% increments, then converted to cover classes
column12: systematic electrofishing at mapped grid points with fish position recorded at first sighting; response variable derived from fish positions was habitat occupany/use
column13: parr avoided areas with no cover (used less than expected based on availability); suitability curves suggested low-to-moderate cover was commonly used, but the low cover preference seemed like it was partly due to limited availabiity; cover interacted with habitat type, especially in "fast" habitat where cover was mainly surface turbulence; PCA did not identify cover as more important than other variables overall, but in fast habitats, cover and velocity were strongly influential
column14: juvenile salmon avoid no-cover areas and use habitats with some cover, but cover acts jointly with velocity and depth rather than as a dominant single driver; primary drivers are mean water velocity, depth, substrate size, and cover with no dominant single variable; consistent avoidance drivers are very low velocity (<10cm/s), very shallow (<10cm) or very deep (>55-60cm) water, fine substrate, and no cover
column15: yes - directly measures and evaluates cover against juvenile fish presence

column6: Seasonal microhabitat use by juvenile spring chinook salmon in the Yakima River basin, Washington
column10: substrate (cobble, boulder, bedrock, gravel, organic detritus), instream objects (cobble, boulders, riparian vegetation, woody debris, "other")
column11: cover was quantified if within 5 ft of observed fish focal position; for each fish focal position, dominant instream cover type within 5 ft was recorded as a categorical variable (presence/absence and type), substrate was recorded in 1ft squared around the focal marker
column12: direct underwater observation (snorkel/SCUBA); divers located undisturbed focal fish and placed a marker at the nose position; after a transect was completed, physical variables were measured at markers; sampling occured in the day (spring, summer, fall) and night (winter)
column13: cover's influence varied seasonally; in spring, 39% of focal positions were associated with woody debris and fry were closely associated with stream margins and cover; in the summer, 51% of fish were more than 5 ft from instream cover with less immediate cover association during active feeding; in fall, there was increased associated with larger substrates (cobble, boulder) and increased use of cover relative to summer; in winter, there was a strong association with dense overwintering cover and contact with substrate
column14: drivers shifted seasonally - cover was especially important during spring and winter but less so during summer; factors included depth (increased with larger size/later season), velocity (higher in summer, very low in winter), substrate size (substrate more important with larger size/later season), instream cover (critical in spring and winter), and distance to bank (offshore movement in summer/fall)
column15: yes - direct measurements of fish location associated with cover, and buffer concept also applied!

column6: Central Valley anadromous salmonid habitat suitability criteria
column10: defined cover coding system (cobble, boulder, fine woody vegetation, fine woody vegetation + overhead, branches, branches + overhead, log, log + overhead, overhead cover, undercut bank, aquatic vegetation, aquatic vegetation + overhead, rip-rap)
column11: "The first step in the development of the cover criteria was to group cover codes, so that there were no significant differences within the groups and a significant difference between the groups, using Pearson’s test for association. Categorical data (substrate and cover) are developed into HSC by calculating the frequencies for each substrate or cover code, and then dividing the frequencies by the highest frequency cover or substrate code, so that the highest HSI value is 1.0. For the Sacramento River, effects of availability on cover use were addressed by subsampling equal areas with and without woody cover... This technique could not be used for the Yuba River and Clear Creek, due to the limited availability of woody cover. As a result, the HSI for each cover group was calculated by dividing the percent of occupied locations in each group by the percent of occupied locations in the group with the highest percent of occupied locations."
column12:
spawning - wading and getting direct measurements at redds (shallow), video (deep)
rearing - snorkel surveys/SCUBA, depending on water depth (dropping numbered tags at fish locations, recording fish size/species/cover code, then measuring depth, velocity, and adjacent velocity at each tag; presence/absence includes sampling unoccupied locations)

column14: "for anadromous salmonids, preferred habitat conditions are assumed to translate into increased growth and survival" (stated but untested assumption); paper suggests that cover allows for food availability via drift, using cover-provided velocity refuges; importantly, this paper evaluates habitat selection but not habitat's influence on fish outcomes (growth, survival, etc.)
